meta.sfa_rationale: The POLITICAL facet of the Roman Republic centres on its distinctive form of government — an aristocratic republic governed through the Senate, magistracies, and popular assemblies — and the political dynamics of its rise, internal contestation, and fall. The scope prioritises governance structures (constitutional history, senatorial authority, citizen assemblies), political rights (civitas, the extension of the franchise), and geopolitical power projection (provincialisation, foreign policy, civil wars) as instruments and expressions of political power. Items primarily belonging to legal doctrine, social stratification, artistic production, or military operations are excluded unless they served as direct mechanisms of political authority — such as quaestiones as political battlegrounds or military imperium as a political resource.

schema_nodes.label: constitutional history
schema_nodes.rationale: Directly traces the evolution of Roman Republican institutions, magistracies, and constitutional arrangements as expressions of political power.

schema_nodes.label: constitutional law
schema_nodes.rationale: Covers the formal structures of Republican governance — consular authority, senatorial supremacy, and the distribution of sovereign power.

schema_nodes.label: civil and political rights
schema_nodes.rationale: Citizenship as a political status (civitas) was the foundational category determining participation in Roman Republican governance.

schema_nodes.label: geopolitics
schema_nodes.rationale: The Republic's expansion, provincial governance, and interstate relations with Carthage, Greece, and the East are core political phenomena.

schema_nodes.label: economic history
schema_nodes.rationale: Included only insofar as fiscal policy, grain supply (annona), and land redistribution debates (Gracchan reforms) were instruments of political contestation.

schema_nodes.label: auxiliary science of history
schema_nodes.rationale: Provides methodological tools (epigraphy, numismatics as political propaganda) for reconstructing Republican political history.

schema_nodes.label: administrative law
schema_nodes.rationale: Provincial administration and magisterial imperium represent political control mechanisms, making administrative law politically relevant here.

schema_nodes.label: Political theory — General and comparative
schema_nodes.rationale: Covers theories of sovereignty, republicanism, and political authority directly applicable to the Roman Republic's governmental model.

schema_nodes.label: Geopolitics
schema_nodes.rationale: Encompasses Roman territorial expansion, hegemony over Italy, and imperial projection as political phenomena.

schema_nodes.label: Civil and political rights
schema_nodes.rationale: Political citizenship (civitas Romana) and its gradual extension were central political events of the Republican period.

schema_nodes.label: Political institutions — Europe
schema_nodes.rationale: Houses scholarship on Roman Republican constitutional arrangements and their European institutional legacy.

schema_nodes.label: History of Italy — Rome (Ancient)
schema_nodes.rationale: The primary LCC location for historical and political narrative of the Roman Republic, including senatorial politics and civil wars.

schema_nodes.label: Law — General
schema_nodes.rationale: Included selectively where law functions as a political weapon — e.g., quaestiones perpetuae and proscription legislation.

schema_nodes.label: Constitutional history and law — Ancient
schema_nodes.rationale: Directly relevant to the formal constitutional arrangements of the Roman Republic including the cursus honorum and tribunicia potestas.

schema_nodes.label: Rome — History — Republic, 510–30 B.C.
schema_nodes.rationale: The canonical LCSH heading precisely delimiting the political history of the Roman Republic as a discrete governmental era.

schema_nodes.label: Constitutional history
schema_nodes.rationale: Covers the historical development of Republican constitutional structures including consuls, Senate, tribunes, and popular assemblies.

schema_nodes.label: Constitutional law
schema_nodes.rationale: Formal legal-political framework of Republican magistracies, vetoes, and the lex as an instrument of political sovereignty.

schema_nodes.label: Civil and political rights
schema_nodes.rationale: Citizenship extension (Social War, Latin rights) was a defining political conflict of the late Republic.

schema_nodes.label: Geopolitics
schema_nodes.rationale: Roman Republican foreign policy, provincial creation, and interstate dominance are geopolitical expressions of political power.

schema_nodes.label: Diplomatic history
schema_nodes.rationale: Treaties, alliances (foedera), and Roman diplomatic supremacy were political instruments of the Republic's power system.

schema_nodes.label: History, Military
schema_nodes.rationale: Included only where military command (imperium) and triumphs functioned as political power resources, not for operational military history per se.

schema_nodes.label: History
schema_nodes.rationale: General historical heading anchoring the Republican period's political narrative within the broader Roman historical record.

schema_nodes.label: Colonies — History
schema_nodes.rationale: Roman colonization was a deliberate political tool for controlling territory and extending citizen communities.

schema_nodes.label: Historical geography
schema_nodes.rationale: The political map of Republican Rome — provincial boundaries, allied territories, and voting districts (tribus) — is essential political geography.

schema_nodes.label: Roman Republic
schema_nodes.rationale: The primary subject entity — an aristocratic republic representing a distinct form of government and historical political period.

schema_nodes.label: Roman Senate
schema_nodes.rationale: The dominant political institution of the Republic, controlling foreign policy, finance, and provincial governance.

schema_nodes.label: Citizens' assemblies of the Roman Republic
schema_nodes.rationale: The comitia and concilium plebis were the sovereign legislative and electoral bodies expressing popular political will.

schema_nodes.label: Roman emperor
schema_nodes.rationale: Relevant as the institutional endpoint whose emergence marks the political collapse of the Republican system.

schema_nodes.label: Ancient Rome
schema_nodes.rationale: The encompassing political civilisation within which the Republican form of government operated and evolved.

schema_nodes.label: Rome
schema_nodes.rationale: The seat of Republican political institutions — Senate, Forum Romanum, and all central magistracies were located here.

schema_nodes.label: Caesar's Civil War
schema_nodes.rationale: The defining political crisis that terminated the Republican constitution and demonstrated the failure of its power-sharing mechanisms.

schema_nodes.label: Ancient Carthage
schema_nodes.rationale: Rome's primary political and military rival, whose defeat structured Roman Republican imperialism and provincial expansion.

schema_nodes.label: Deposition of Romulus Augustulus
schema_nodes.rationale: Marks the formal end of the Western Roman political tradition descended from the Republic, relevant as a terminal political boundary.

schema_nodes.label: Carthage
schema_nodes.rationale: The city whose political destruction (146 BCE) marked Rome's unchallenged political hegemony in the Mediterranean.

schema_nodes.label: Athens
schema_nodes.rationale: Politically significant as a model for Republican constitutional thought and as a subject of Roman political subordination after 146 BCE.

schema_nodes.label: Byzantium
schema_nodes.rationale: Relevant as a Greek polis under Republican political control and later as the site of imperial political continuity.
